content:
I analysed the 100 most recent UK funding announcements on Soapbox. Nearly a third described their product as:

• "The [something] layer for [sector]"
• "Intelligence layer"
• "Infrastructure layer"
• "The operating system for [X]"
• "Agentic platform"

These aren't companies in the same space saying similar things. It's photonics and quantum and drug discovery and real estate and payments, all reaching for the same handful of words. A company putting laser comms in orbit and a company doing SME accounting using the same sentence structure to describe themselves.

The short of it is: that's investor language. It works in a pitch room.

"The intelligence layer for X" tells a VC: we're infrastructure, not a point solution. Bigger TAM, more defensible, harder to replace. That's a meaningful signal when you're trying to raise.

But investor messaging and marketing messaging serve different purposes.

A funding announcement might be the first time a prospect, a potential hire, or a future partner discovers your company. It's high-reach, high-attention, and it doesn't repeat. "The intelligence layer for X" tells them you're not sure who your customer really is.

Your customer is not thinking about TAM or defensibility. They have a problem. They need to know if you can solve it.

The tell is often a confused prospect. Or, worse, a confused team. If your GTM team are raising their shoulders wondering what you're talking about a few days before the release goes out, you're missing a moment you won't get again in a long time.

Your funding announcement is a big brand opportunity, so you shouldn't treat it as an investor update.

Before the next one goes out, ask yourself: does your positioning speak to the people who will make you successful, or the people who already said yes?

More insights from this research to come >>

content:
The best time to build the team you want is before you're too busy to do it right.

Watching Citadelle grow over the past few months has been incredibly rewarding. It's proved something I’ve always believed in: founder-led content, clear brand positioning and a purposeful go to market strategy can turn even the most technical ideas into real pipeline — if you do it thoughtfully.

And Founders and VCs tell me the same thing again and again: there’s a real gap for fractional Marketing & GTM support that goes beyond fancy decks and motivational advisory services.

What they want is still rare: senior operators who can think strategically and deliver with pace and precision like there’s no tomorrow. 

Because in most early-stage startups, unless you deliver, there really is no tomorrow.

So when momentum started to build, I doubled down with intention. Which is why I’m delighted to welcome Imogen Lees to Citadelle! 

With 10+ years in Product and B2B marketing, launching high-revenue propositions, building engaged communities, scaling agencies like Planes Studio to become industry-leading thought leaders, she’s the rare senior marketer who imagines strategically and executes with urgency. 

She’s exactly the calibre you should expect from us: strategic maturity and operational grit in equal measure. And she’s a delightful thought partner, so how much luckier can Camilla and I get? 

We’re growing Citadelle, not because we have to, but because we get to. I can’t wait to see where the next months take us as a team of three 🚀

content:
Your clients and partners don’t want you at Cannes Lions. They want you, present and engaged, in their strategy workshops, at their roadmap meetings…

And, if you’re lucky enough, they want you at their annual team retreats too.

It’s been over a year since I started as StackOne’s fractional Head of Marketing, so I’m feeling super grateful that I got to celebrate this milestone on their annual offsite in Porto last week. 

I get tons of energy from collaborating in person, so both the work and the fun parts (IE paddle boarding against the current in the Portuguese sun) were a delight. 

Working with StackOne has been a real privilege, because it's so rare to get to work with people who are just as kind as they are brilliant. I’ve supported Romain, Guillaume, Richard and the team in some of the most pressing areas of GTM, translating deep technical debates into compelling brand stories and turning our content into a proper thought leadership machine. 

And all of this is only possible when you earn the trust to be invited in the room where the real work happens (hey, Hamilton reference). 

Thank you for having me team, here’s to another year of showing up & building rocket ships together 🚀

And huge thanks to Isobel Sprules who organised the mother of all offsites for 20 people, this woman is a powerhouse 👏🏻 

P.S. StackOne is currently hiring for a number of roles, I'll drop their careers page in the comments for those in my network who are interested ⬇️

content:
I just wrapped up an incredibly energising GTM project, helping Clue build the foundation of their Partnerships growth engine.

When a household brand like Clue moves into strategic partnerships, their challenge isn’t growth. It’s precision. 

How do you extend a loved consumer product into new B2B-driven growth channels, without diluting the brand or losing focus of your mission and values? 

Over the last few months, I had the genuine privilege of:
• Designing Clue’s partnerships GTM strategy from the ground up
• Validating their ICPs across femtech, wellbeing and other key sectors
• Crafting modular messaging frameworks tailored to specific buying dynamics
• Launching a LinkedIn thought leadership strategy to drive inbound traction 
• Building a commercial pipeline and onboarding playbook for new partners

The lesson I keep coming back to at the end of this project is simple: a good GTM strategy is one that’s built fast, tested live and iterated thoughtfully and continuously with customer feedback. It's also one that retains the essence and the mission behind the brand and the values of those who lead it. 

Thank you Balint Veres, Rhiannon White, Hugo Marshall and Louise Troen for your trust and collaboration. What you’re building in the reproductive health space deserves world recognition and I’m so excited to see how you go on to achieve it! 

And thank you Camilla for being such a great thought partner and helping me unlock some thorny challenges along the way. 

If you’re a founder navigating a similar shift (from B2C to B2B or from product-market fit to partner-market fit) and you need advice on how to cut the noise and get you in market the right way & at the right time, drop me a note!

content:
Couldn't be more proud of the StackOne team for their amazing $20M Series A achievement! 

Some milestones speak for themselves, especially when you're backed by industry leaders like GV (Google Ventures), Workday Ventures and XTX Ventures. 

I rarely get to talk about the challenges of marketing a complex technical product, especially when the speed of innovation moves so much faster than the wider market, so it's even more rewarding to see StackOne get a moment in the spotlight today on Sifted's Top Stories 🚀
 
Congratulations Romain Sestier, Guillaume Lebedel and the StackOne team, can't wait for where we're headed next!

repost.content:
We’re thrilled to announce StackOne’s $20M Series A round — led by GV (Google Ventures), with participation from Workday Ventures, XTX Ventures, Episode 1 Ventures and Playfair and support from angels at OpenAI, Deepmind, Microsoft, Mulesoft and more 🚀

At Google, Yieldify, Oracle and in our first startup, Guillaume Lebedel and I saw Product teams burn months wrestling with dozens of point-to-point integrations, hiring entire squads just to maintain them. Meanwhile, the rise of AI Agents is creating an entirely new generation of software that demands new kinds of real-time, bidirectional integrations.

Fast forward to today, with StackOne, we’re reinventing how integrations are built altogether. We have built a proprietary AI engine that writes deep, deterministic, enterprise-ready integrations at scale, breaking the limits of the previous generation of integration platforms. And StackOne is AI-native: it offers both API and AI interfaces like MCP that enable SaaS and AI Agents to integrate deeply into enterprise products more accurately and quickly than with the leading LLM models.

I’m excited about the road ahead and grateful to our investors, our team and our customers for believing and investing in this vision. We can’t wait to show you what’s in store this year & beyond!

Read more in our press release and coverage and join our webinar next week, where we dive deep into our vision for the future of integrations.

🔗 Press release: https://lnkd.in/eiQ5KV43

📆 Webinar: https://lu.ma/ehids7kw

----------------------------------------

Thank you to all our investors, advisors, and early believers! Luna Schmid, Barbry McGann, Gabriele Papievyte, Hector Mason, Henrik Wetter Sanchez, Andrew Sheffield, Bradley Horowitz, Laura Modiano, ☁️Henrique Moniz de Aragão, Valentino Pacifici, Daniel Levine, Zachary Gleicher, Michelangelo Pagliara, Mike Saparov, Raanan Raz, Anneka Gupta, Misha Jessel-Kenyon, Jim Griffin, Trevor Brown, Revenue Syndicate, Charlie Songhurst, Oliver Molander, Rajeeb Dey MBE, Jay Radia and Blissgrowth and so many more!

content:
And then there were two 🚀

I'm excited to welcome Camilla Guida as Citadelle’s new Marketing & Growth Manager! 

As many of you know, I started this journey as a one-woman band. Until recently, I’d never used the Citadelle name or spoke about myself as an agency founder — despite that being my ambition for the past two years. 

But that changes today.

While it’s not lost on me how privileged I’ve been to work on my own terms with some incredible clients and brands, it’s time to build on the success I’ve achieved so far, invest in growth and take the work to the next level.

Enter Camilla! 

She’s one of those unicorns, whose grit and flair made her speedy ascent in the world of start-up marketing & growth seem the most natural thing (when it was anything but!). So I’m thrilled to be in a position to welcome her to the team.

We have some incredible projects lined up that I can’t wait to share more about in the coming months. 

And as for who we are collectively, Citadelle’s mission is to empower founders with bold marketing that builds memorable brands and drives results. We’ve been working with Seed to Series A+ start-ups and their VC partners to accelerate the 0-1 journey and we get tons of energy from solving complex problems that need marketable solutions. 

If that’s you or this sounds interesting, stay tuned to see where we’re headed 🚀

content:
Ever wondered how tyre maker Michelin revolutionised fine dining? 

Tyres and gourmet cuisine might seem like an unlikely pairing, but their story is a masterclass in brand legacy and content marketing. 

Back in 1900, Michelin brothers André and Édouard faced a problem: cars were rare, roads were rough and their tyre business needed to generate demand. 

So they came up with a solution, to create a free booklet packed with: 

🗺️ Useful maps
🛠️ Car repair tips
🍣 Curated lists of hotels & restaurants

All with a single-minded goal: getting people to drive more. 

More miles = more tyre wear = more sales. Simple, genius and rooted in razor-sharp brand strategy.

And thus, The Michelin Guide was born. And here’s the real genius behind it:

→They built an ecosystem around their product. Instead of just selling tyres and promoting features, they sold the idea of adventure, making car travel aspirational.

→They evolved with purpose. In 1926, they added the iconic star ratings.  Overnight, a travel guide morphed into the global authority on fine dining. A tyre company was now the arbiter of culinary excellence! 

→ By 1920, they began charging for the guide, boosting its perceived value. Anonymous inspectors then ensured that every recommendation lived up to the Michelin promise. 

Two critical lessons here for us brand builders:

1. The best marketing strategies solve a customer problem. Michelin didn’t advertise tyres, they created experiences that made people get out of their seats and onto the road. 

2. Brands become legendary when they outgrow their original purpose. What started as a sales tactic is now a cultural institution, proving that bold, long-term thinking can turn utility into legacy.

Marketing is all about building ecosystems, crafting narratives and creating moments that resonate with people (see how I didn’t say “consumers”?). 

It's only when we design experiences with purpose that brands can become timeless.

This is by far the one example I keep going back to as I think about long-term brand growth. 

What's yours?

content:
Have you ever had a moment that forever changed the way you think about brand?
 
For me, it was winning the £1M Sky Zero Footprint Fund with Here We Flo. It wasn’t just the prize (although that was a wild experience in itself), but rather the moment I realised the true power of a “brand moment”.
 
Before going on stage to announce that we won, Sir John Hegarty turned to another finalist and said, jokingly “Should’ve worn a pink dress!”
 
His words stuck with me. Because the truth is, a brand is all about the moments that people remember.
 
Our £1M win was the culmination of an incredibly passionate team coming together to create magic. But a key part of that breakthrough came from one suggestion I had made: let’s all wear pink dresses and show up as if we had already won.
 
Even early in my career, I understood that we get endless opportunities to create “brand moments”, and they don’t need big budgets. They come from bold ideas and showing up with confidence.
 
Hearing Sir John Hegarty’s words made me realise that a brand is really the sum of all these moments we get to create. The moments that make people feel something.
 
And what a privilege to be on-stage presenting alongside Susan C. Allen Augustin as Tara Chandra’s baby made an early entrance into the world! Needless to say, there was never a dull moment at Here We Flo.

content:
In 2024, I took a leap of faith — and it paid off. 

Six years in the start-up trenches taught me one thing: early-stage founders need a marketer who can do it all. High-level strategy, hands-on execution and everything in-between. 

Why?
 
Hiring a full-time CMO early on is very costly, and their main focus is understandably on high-level strategy. And even the most ambitious junior operators will burn out without the right support to guide them through the complexities of scaling. 

That’s where I come in. 

Last year, I decided to back myself and go fractional, bringing my "full-stack marketer" skills to early-stage founders and start-ups who need a strategic and executional powerhouse.

In under six months, I: 
• Developed a content strategy for a $1B tech company, leveraging their C-suite for the first time
• Turned LinkedIn into a growth engine for four founders, 5x’ing their reach and 2x’ing their followers
• Built a community of 100+ thought leaders through curated events
• Out-earned my highest full-time salary in record time 

Behind the scenes, I wore every hat: content creator, strategist, project manager, events coordinator, the list goes on. But what I was really doing was building something that feels purposeful and exciting. Something I'm excited to be scaling this year. 

Now, I’m heading into 2025 with more focus and energy than ever.

This year, I’m doubling down on what I do best: helping founders, start-ups and hands-on investors build brands that make them feel unstoppable.

If you’re looking for someone who’s been forged in the fires of the start-up world — someone who can strategise, execute & navigate the chaos with you — drop me a note! 

Excited to build the unstoppable brands of 2025 🚀

Last night, I got to attend the BFI world premiere of Joy alongside the brilliant Apricity Fertility team and their community partners.

It's an incredible film about the drive and determination that led to the birth of IVF. 

It's also a beautiful story about the relentless belief that people deserve a meaningful choice on their path to parenthood, and a brilliant testament to the scientific advances that helped over 10 million children come into the world through assisted reproduction.

I rarely, if ever, get to walk the red carpet for work, so here's to Apricity Fertility for creating such a special moment! 

I couldn't think of a better event to capture Apricity's mission and genuine efforts to making fertility care accessible and affordable.

content:
Wearing too many hats as a Founder-CEO-CMO? It’s probably time to rethink your approach to Marketing.

In the early stages of your startup, you’re not just the visionary behind your product—you’re also the CMO, often by default. You’ve got strong opinions about who your customers are, how your brand should sound, and what messages need to get out there. 

And that’s worked—so far.

But here’s the thing: as your business grows, this approach can start to hold you back. When your brand’s tone of voice becomes synonymous with your own, it can stifle creativity, lead to clashes over content decisions, and make it tough to collaborate effectively with your team or external partners.

The challenge? You risk building a brand that’s so closely tied to you that it becomes subjective and difficult to scale. And when your marketing strategy is too reliant on your personal views, it can create barriers to growth and limit the potential of your marketing efforts.

So, how do you break free and set your brand up for scalable success?

Here’s how you can start empowering your team and partners to take your marketing to the next level:

1. Download your brain
Create a simple 1-pager that captures how you’ve approached marketing so far. Outline your positioning, channels, what’s worked, what hasn’t, and why. This document becomes the foundation for your team to build on.

2. Set clear briefs
At the start of every project, provide your team, agencies, or consultants with a consistent, thorough brief. This ensures everyone’s on the same page and can collaborate effectively, no matter their expertise.

3. Ask and listen
Encourage open dialogue. Ask questions, provide feedback early, and be willing to debate when someone suggests a better way. This not only fosters creativity but also helps refine your brand’s direction.

4. Let go
This is probably the toughest one, but it's crucial to recognise that as your company grows, so should your brand identity—beyond just your own vision. Trust in your team’s expertise and be open to new ideas that align with your overall business goals.

Thoughts?

content:
The biggest mistake your brand can make is patronising your customer. 

In the rush to create buzz or be controversial, many brands end up alienating the people they’re aiming to reach. 

Most often this happens for four reasons:

1. Their perceived target audience does not align with their actual audience 

2. A reflection of an industry that lacks diversity of experience and thought, as most marketers come from a specific background (more on that in another post)

3. They pay big agencies for half-baked ideas and brand managers don’t challenge them against their audience and values

4. A deliberate choice along the lines of “all PR is good PR”, which is plain lazy

I’ve seen great campaigns that have one terrible execution amidst a few good ones, which usually means the bad one gets brushed aside as a mistake without accountability. I’ve also seen awful campaigns that should have never seen the light of day (Pepsi’s Kendal Jenner ad - oof). 

Here’s a case in point:  

“You know full well a vow of celibacy is not the answer.” 

“Thou shalt not give up on dating and become a nun.” 

This was Bumble’s response to a cultural movement that gained traction earlier this year in the US. 

The reason it’s patronising is fairly obvious: they took a deeply sensitive topic and turning it into a leisurely joke. 

It speaks volumes that no one in the room where this campaign was approved flagged it. They didn’t need a celibate person to tell them this was a poor move for a dating app claiming to empower women. 

But Bumble did turn it around and apologised for the oversight, which not many brands do. They listened and learned, and I doubt they’ll make this mistake again. 

If you represent or own a brand, here’s my top tip:

Respect your audience's intelligence and cultural nuances and prioritise genuine connection over shock value. 

The cost of infantilising or underestimating your audience’s intelligence can be heftier than you imagine in the long run, because tone-deaf advertising damages your reputation and puts the trust people might have in your brand at risk. 

A well-thought-out campaign that resonates on a deeper level will pay off more in the long run over a carelessly controversial one.

Thoughts?

content:
What do you get when you release 100+ marketers into a field, free-range style? 

They optimise the grazing experience for maximum ROI (great punchline, sit down ChatGPT…) 

Jokes aside, I had an insightful day at Up World's Up Fest last week. I met some interesting people, had delicious farm-to-table food — all the jazz.

Here are my favourite learnings:

🤝 Marketing and Growth have a meaningful opportunity to learn from each other. Applying Growth experiments to market and consumer research will help you learn faster; bringing Brand people into Growth testing will ensure your message doesn't get diluted in the iteration process 

📉 VCs care about intellectual honesty in your pitch deck. If you can’t anticipate and outline an action plan for when your CACs plateau or start rising (they inevitably do), you can miss out on meaningful investment opportunities.

🌍 Not every product needs to go mass-market, not every brand needs to have a D2C offering. If you’re selling a low-margin product, D2C alone is probably not the channel for you. 

🎨 Your brand is as strong as your next activation. You don’t need 10 USPs to resonate, just one that’s deeply rooted in how your customers feel about the problem you’re trying to solve / the experience you’re creating.

💗 Emotion drives action, you can’t bore people into buying your product by listing facts about it. 

Thank you Nicholas Rooney, Jake H., Andrew Walker, Kevin Chesters for some brilliant discussions on the above. 

Something I've been reflecting on after the event was how respect for marketing as a discipline can vary significantly across industries and is often influenced by the quality and impact of the work we produce. This was a powerful reminder of the value we bring and how much we can learn from and amplify each other. 

Thank you Hope Kelly, Lottie Unwin (she/her) and the Up World team for a great experience!

actor.name: Diandra Dumitru
actor.position: Fractional Marketing & GTM | Turning complex products into category leaders.

content:
You won’t change people’s behaviour with facts.

In fact, you’re 22 times 𝐥𝐞𝐬𝐬 𝐥𝐢𝐤𝐞𝐥𝐲 to change their behaviour through facts than you are through storytelling. 

Why?

Because our interpretation of the world is heavily context-mediated. How you portray something directly impacts how people perceive it, and perception is highly emotional.

Take these examples:

1. The "Click It or Ticket" campaign has been highly effective by using personal stories and emotional appeals to encourage seat belt use, rather than just presenting statistics. This approach significantly increased seat belt usage across the U.S.

2. Back Market’s “Monster" tells the tale of a young girl who faces a tech-obsessed creature, highlighting the absurdity of producing countless slightly improved devices to satisfy our endless craving for the latest gadgets.

3. Instead of bombarding us with health or exercise-related stats or clichés every January, Gym Box are known to get real about the chaos that is modern life, giving people the feeling of “huh! they get it.” 

For marketers, this shows the power of narrative in getting your customers to act on, not just witness, your brand. 

And despite what the majority of B2B marketing out there will have you thinking, this part is as important in B2B as it is in B2C — I’ve said this before and I’ll say it again: people buy from people, not from brands. 

Compelling brand stories is what drives action. Instead of inundating your audience with data or superfluous information, focus on weaving narratives that resonate emotionally and align with their values.

Here are the top three channels that can benefit from smart storytelling and that you can act on right now:  

1. Social media: platforms like Instagram and LinkedIn are perfect for sharing engaging stories that highlight your brand’s journey, values, and customer success stories
2. Content marketing: blogs, articles and videos that tell compelling stories can attract and retain your audience, driving organic traffic and building loyalty 
3. Email: personalised stories in email campaigns can foster a deeper connection with your audience, leading to higher open and conversion rates

I quite like Rory Sutherland's take on this: a fact becomes a brand message when you carefully craft how it will be perceived. 

What do you think?

actor.position: Marketing, Sales and Agentic AI

content:
Marketers, your most valuable skill in the AI era is strategic thinking. You don’t need to be a Head of anything to master it. 

Not everyone is responsible for crafting a high-level strategy, but everyone, at every level, is responsible for strategic thinking. 

I see it time and time again: investors have an idea on how a business should be marketed, the founders bring it to you and you start executing it blindly. 

I’ve been there too. 

But judgement is crucial in every aspect of our work, and knowing your customer inside-out is your job. Execution without judgement weakens long-term success, leading to projects that don’t align with your goals, thus diluting the quality of work in higher-impact areas.

It’s not just your annual goals or OKRs that need to illustrate your customer focus or commercial awareness, but every single touch point you have with your target audience. Strategic thinking is an all day every day process, not just annually or quarterly. It involves (the hard task of) challenging initiatives, wherever they come from, if they won’t get you where you’re headed. It demands being proactive and rigorous in reviewing every.single.marketing. channel, always. 

Your role as a marketer is to ensure every activity or customer touch point serves your overarching objectives.

I really enjoyed Marketing Against the Grain's episode on this topic with Wes Kao, an important one for marketers at any stage of their career. 

Thoughts?

actor.name: Shashank Mishra
actor.position: Content strategist and writer for B2B, dev tools, and tech brands

content:
If you’re using social media to shove company info down people’s throats, you’re going at it the wrong way — and it's why you’re not succeeding. 

People buy from people — not companies, not brands.

This applies as much to B2B SaaS as it does to FMCG. 

People love companies like innocent drinks, SURREAL and HubSpot because each of them found a way to breathe life into their brands by featuring a founder, a boss or a cheeky marketing team that embodies their values and personality in a way that resonates on each platform they’re active on. 

innocent drinks aren’t afraid to get playful and post about the intrusive thoughts we all get during the work day. And they do it unashamedly in all caps. 

SURREAL are owning being a scrappy start-up with a marketing team that *really* doesn’t like being told what to do. They’re hilarious, rebellious and memorable. 

HubSpot speak to Gen Z-ers with a love of emojis and puns on marketing terminology. They’re relatable and useful for social media folk. 

It works. 

Each post about their products occasionally. They even share updates about new jobs or company events. But their primary focus is relating to their audience, putting them first, engaging with what resonates with them...not pushing products or features out mindlessly. 

They’re adding value to their audiences’ lives, because ultimately, your brand isn’t — or shouldn’t be — about you. 

It should be about your community.  

#brand #marketing #toneofvoice #knowyouraudience #linkedinstrategy
